bull_thesis:
Gartner (IT) at $160.01: a high-quality, cash-generating research franchise that the market has mispriced by treating a temporary slowdown as permanent decline.

Why the price looks wrong. Gartner earns a 67.7% gross margin and an 18.1% free-cash-flow (cash earnings after all capital spending) margin. It trades at roughly 9.8 times free cash flow — a multiple normally reserved for businesses that are shrinking for good. The internal rate of return on capital (ROIC, a measure of how profitably the company reinvests) sits at 21%, and management cites a rolling figure closer to 27%. A business with those economics does not deserve a 10x free-cash-flow price tag unless investors have concluded the model is permanently broken. The bull case is that they have made exactly that mistake.

The slowdown is driven by external shocks, not product failure. Contract value (CV, the annualised value of all active subscription contracts and the single most important forward-looking metric for a subscription business) fell from roughly 8% growth in late 2024 to 1% by the first quarter of 2026. That six-quarter slide maps precisely onto a sequence of identifiable macro events, not onto any product deterioration: government spending cuts under the DOGE programme hit Gartner's federal client base hard (management renewed only roughly half of federal business in early 2025, a one-time step down that will eventually lap); tariff uncertainty crushed CEO confidence, which by mid-2025 had fallen to levels described as "among the fastest drops ever recorded"; and by early 2026, clients were explicitly citing geopolitics as the reason for deferring decisions. Gartner sells discretionary advisory subscriptions — the first line item a CFO defers when confidence collapses. When confidence recovers, deferrals reverse. A subscription business that loses 700 basis points of growth in six quarters on product failure looks very different from one that loses it in a synchronised buyer-confidence freeze, which is what the transcript record describes.

Pricing power is the clearest sign the moat is intact. Gartner has raised list prices roughly 4% per year for more than 15 years with, in management's words, "minimal pushback." If large-language models were genuinely substituting for Gartner's research, the first symptom would be pricing collapse — clients would demand discounts at renewal or simply leave. Instead, increases are still landing. A rumour of sub-$50,000 average-selling-price discounting surfaced in the first quarter of 2026 and was flatly denied by the CFO: "we do not offer discounts." Fifteen years of uninterrupted price increases through the worst confidence environment in the dataset is hard evidence that clients still consider the product worth paying for.

The growth deceleration is a volume problem, not a value problem — and volume is cyclical. The clients who are leaving are disproportionately small technology vendors: the most cash-constrained, macro-sensitive, venture-funding-dependent segment of Gartner's book. Core enterprise-function leaders (chief information officers, chief financial officers, supply-chain heads) are not churning at the same rate; that cohort's CV grew 10% as recently as mid-2024. The addressable market has not shrunk — Gartner estimates roughly 140,000 potential enterprise clients, of which only about 14,000 are currently subscribers, a penetration rate of around 10%. When management reports that subscription upgrades and downgrades "balance out almost exactly," that is consistent with a paused but loyal core base, not with the pattern you would expect from a franchise in secular decline.

Buying back shares at 10x free cash flow is the highest-returning use of capital available. The company repurchased $735 million of stock in 2024, more than $2 billion in 2025, and $535 million in the first quarter of 2026 alone, reducing the share count by roughly 8% cumulatively and 4% in a single quarter. To fund additional repurchases, it issued its first investment-grade bond in late 2025. At a 9.8x price-to-free-cash-flow multiple, retiring equity that yields roughly 10% is rational capital allocation. Net debt of $3.25 billion is comfortably covered by roughly $1.18 billion in annual free cash flow. This is the same playbook used by other high-return, low-growth annuities: shrink the share count steadily and let per-share value compound even if total earnings are flat.

The reported earnings collapse is largely a comparison problem. Net income fell 39.7% in 2025 (from $1.25 billion to $729 million, EPS from $16.00 to $9.65), which sounds alarming. But the 2024 base was artificially inflated by a one-time tax-planning benefit that added 79% to fourth-quarter 2024 earnings per share. Stripping that out, the underlying earnings trajectory is much gentler. Free cash flow margin of 18.1% still exceeds the net margin of 11.2%, confirming that cash earnings are structurally stronger than reported profits. On the metrics management controls directly — beating consensus estimates — execution has been flawless: the company has beaten earnings expectations in every one of the past seven quarters, following a consistent pattern of setting guidance with room for upside. Management's own forward earnings-per-share target of more than 12% annual growth over three years is consistent with the consensus forecast of 12.5% and is achievable through share-count reduction and cost flexibility alone, before any CV recovery is needed.

Insiders are buying. The ratio of insider purchases to sales stands at 3.47 — a meaningful signal from the people who have access to the detailed substitution data that the company declines to publish publicly. Insiders do not accumulate stock at that rate into a franchise they believe is in structural decline.

The artificial-intelligence product pivot is showing real results. AskGartner, an AI assistant built on Gartner's proprietary research corpus, has been described by management as "the single largest demand area," and — crucially — early data show that clients who adopt it renew at higher rates. Rather than losing ground to general-purpose AI tools, Gartner appears to be using the same technology to make its own product more valuable. Proprietary peer-benchmarking data, direct analyst access, and a deeply embedded renewal relationship are the elements of the moat that a public large-language model cannot replicate.

For the bull case to hold, five conditions must remain true: (1) CV growth re-accelerates as the federal headwind laps and CEO confidence recovers — the first quarter of 2026 already showed a nominal uptick from the fourth quarter of 2025, the first directional improvement; (2) the roughly 4% annual price increases continue to land without meaningful resistance; (3) share repurchases continue at more than 5% of the float per year, mechanically delivering the 12%-plus earnings-per-share growth target independent of revenue; (4) free-cash-flow conversion stays above 100% of net income and gross margin stays near 67%; and (5) AskGartner's renewal-rate benefit broadens. If those conditions hold, an investor at $160 is buying a roughly 10% free-cash-flow yield with a 38% gap to estimated fair value of $220.69, an insider-buy ratio of 3.47, and an option on CV recovery at no additional cost.
bear_thesis:
Gartner (IT) at $160.01: a franchise whose management has promised a recovery for six consecutive quarters without delivering one, now using debt-funded share buybacks to manufacture per-share earnings growth as the core product faces its first credible long-term threat.

The central problem. The company's own internal analysis, reflected in the earnings-call dossier, states the growth engine "has materially stalled" and that "management continues to narrate a recovery that the numbers have not yet delivered for six straight quarters." Contract value (CV, the annualised value of all active subscriptions) is the leading indicator for a subscription business. It fell from roughly 8% growth in late 2024 to 1% by the first quarter of 2026 — a clean, monotonic deceleration in the one metric that predicts future revenue.

Management called the bottom wrong, repeatedly. In mid-2024, the CFO declared that "the first quarter marked the bottom for CV growth this cycle." It was not the bottom; CV kept falling. Management then promised a "clear path back to double-digit growth" for six consecutive quarters without delivering it. The dossier's own assessment of management's forward CV guidance is "low reliability." An investor cannot build a cyclical-recovery thesis on the word of a team whose growth forecasts have a documented zero-for-six track record.

The earnings collapse is real and the peak was partly artificial. Full-year 2025 net income fell 39.7%, from $1.25 billion to $729 million, with earnings per share dropping from $16.00 to $9.65. The bull correctly notes that the 2024 base was inflated by a one-time tax benefit — but that cuts both ways: it means the 2024 peak of $16.00 per share was itself partially artificial, making the entire multi-year growth record look flatter than the headline numbers. The long-run data bear this out: book value per share has grown 0.8% annually over five years, earnings per share 1.2% annually, and the three-year earnings-per-share compound annual growth rate is negative 1.0%. A business compounding earnings at 1.2% per year over five years is not a compounder — it is a flat annuity dressed in subscription-quality optics. The widely cited return on equity of 203.7% is an artifact of a shrinking equity base and $3.25 billion in net debt, not evidence of high organic reinvestment returns.

The biggest risk is the one management refuses to discuss. Multiple analysts have asked, quarter after quarter, whether clients are replacing Gartner with public large-language models. Management calls it "essentially unmeasurable" and "extremely small." Gartner's core product is curated, written research synthesis and best-practice advice — precisely the cognitive task at which frontier AI models have improved fastest. The entire deceleration arc from 2024 to 2026 coincides with the rapid enterprise adoption of capable generative AI. Describing the impact as immeasurable for eight straight quarters, while declining to commission or publish any substitution data, is not the behaviour of a management team confident the answer is benign.

AI is a double-edged sword management only presents from one side. Management positions AI as its primary demand driver, but the same technology is the most credible threat to Gartner's written-research moat. AskGartner may also be the mechanism by which clients discover they can get most of the value from a general-purpose model at a fraction of the cost. The renewal-rate correlation management cites is self-reported and unaudited, appearing at exactly the moment the headline key performance indicator needed a positive offset.

The narrative shows the classic signs of a company managing perception rather than diagnosing a problem. The dossier identifies four patterns: first, key performance indicator substitution — as CV decelerated, management introduced activity metrics (documents produced up 19%, publishing time down 75%, AI conversation volumes) instead of outcome metrics; second, goalpost creep — the concrete "12% to 16% CV growth" medium-term target was quietly dropped; third, segment renaming — the Research segment was renamed "Business and Technology Insights" in mid-2025, and non-subscription revenue was reclassified into "other revenue" shortly after, changes that can obscure trend comparability during a slowdown; fourth, scripted closings — management's closing remarks have been nearly verbatim across multiple quarters, suggesting message discipline rather than candid reassessment.

The buyback is financial engineering, not value creation, and it is being funded with debt. At a 9.8x price-to-free-cash-flow multiple the buyback is mathematically accretive only if the business is not structurally impaired. If CV at 1% is the leading edge of secular decline, management is using the durable free cash flow of a melting business to retire shares on the way down. Worse, issuing an investment-grade bond specifically "to support even more share repurchase capacity" converts a clean balance sheet into a leveraged bet on the cyclical thesis being correct. The "12% earnings-per-share CAGR" target is achievable only through share-count reduction and easy year-over-year comparisons, not through organic earnings growth — it is an arithmetic outcome of financial engineering, not evidence of a healthy franchise.

Margins have already moved lower. Management conceded in late 2025 that 23.5% EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation) margin is "the new baseline" — a confirmed permanent step-down from the prior roughly 25% level. Free-cash-flow growth is already negative 15% year over year. The gross margin of 67.7% is intact for now, but operating margin compression in a "much tougher selling environment" where "the value bar is higher" is historically how a deteriorating franchise begins to crack.

Client-count erosion is the persistent, unresolved structural concern. Analyst after analyst has pressed on it — Silber, Bibb, Haas — every quarter, without a satisfying resolution. The count keeps declining, perpetually attributed to "small tech vendor churn." A subscription franchise's intrinsic value is the number of active clients multiplied by CV per client; if client count is falling and CV per client growth has stalled at 1%, both components of that equation are moving in the wrong direction simultaneously. A rumour of discounting to below $50,000 in average deal size surfaced in the first quarter of 2026 and, while denied, signals that the sales force is under real pressure.

The bear case is confirmed if: CV fails to re-accelerate in the second or third quarter of 2026; the roughly 4% annual price increase meets real resistance or is substantiated by field-level discounting; EBITDA margin takes another step below the conceded 23.5% baseline; free cash flow declines further, slowing the buyback that is the only lever producing the 12% earnings-per-share growth target; client count continues to fall while down-selling starts to outweigh upgrades; or any disclosure — voluntary or forced — puts a number on AI substitution that is non-trivial. In that scenario, the 38% "margin of safety" to estimated fair value proves illusory, built on a valuation model that assumed a growth recovery that never arrived, and the debt-funded buyback will have spent the balance sheet buying stock on the way down.
consensus_delta:
Where we disagree with the market (adjusted for the macro environment as of 5 June 2026: a late-cycle environment with unresolved oil and inflation pressures — West Texas Intermediate crude near $92, consumer price index re-accelerated to 3.8%, the Federal Reserve on hold through roughly December, and a higher-for-longer interest-rate backdrop).

The core question is whether the market is pricing in permanent decline for a company with stable or growing earnings power. The answer is: partly, but the market is not obviously wrong.

Where the market is right. The street correctly identifies that the franchise has stalled. Contract value decelerated monotonically from roughly 8% growth in late 2024 to 1% by the first quarter of 2026. The long-run numbers confirm the damage is not an optical illusion: five-year earnings-per-share growth of 1.2%, five-year book-value-per-share growth of 0.8%, a three-year earnings-per-share compound annual growth rate of negative 1.0%, free cash flow down 15% year over year, and full-year 2025 net income down 39.7%. The portion of consensus that says "this is a stalled franchise, not a secular compounder" is factually correct.

Where the market might be wrong. The one genuine area where the market may be too pessimistic is the implied growth rate baked into a roughly 9.8x price-to-free-cash-flow multiple. At that price, for a business with a 67.7% gross margin, 21% return on capital, free-cash-flow conversion above 100%, and fifteen years of uninterrupted roughly 4% annual price increases with minimal pushback, the market is implicitly pricing permanent zero real growth or outright decline. A buyer of roughly 8% of the float each year mechanically compounds per-share value in the low double digits even at flat revenue — and the consensus forward earnings-per-share growth forecast of 12.5% corroborates that arithmetic.

Why this is not a clean opportunity. The central variable that would tell us whether the slowdown is cyclical or structural is not discoverable by either side: management has declined for eight straight quarters to disclose any data on how often clients cite large-language models as an alternative. An unresolvable central question is not an edge — it is a coin toss priced roughly fairly.

The macro environment makes the bull's case harder, not easier, on two counts. First, the bull's payoff depends on the oil-and-inflation-driven CEO-confidence channel recovering, but that channel is still adverse: a 3.8% consumer price index reading, roughly $92 oil, and a Federal Reserve on hold means enterprises remain in cost-cutting mode — the precise condition that keeps Gartner's most-deferrable subscriptions deferred. Second, the AI-spending theme is the dominant market factor for 2026, and AI investment is roughly 40% of expected 2026 earnings-per-share growth across the market. Any news that quantifies AI substitution of Gartner's product as non-trivial would be an immediate negative, in a tape that is maximally sensitive to exactly that theme. The bull needs both of these factors to turn favourable; the macro environment says they are, if anything, turning less favourable near-term.

Net assessment. The market is not clearly looking the wrong way. It is pricing a genuinely ambiguous franchise at a multiple that is cheap if the slowdown is cyclical and roughly fair if it is structural. The late-cycle, oil-shocked, higher-for-longer backdrop tilts the near-term odds toward the "still deferring" outcome, not toward the recovery the cheap multiple requires. That is a fairly-priced unresolved question made more adverse by the current tape, not a captured mispricing.
forcing_function:
The catalyst that forces the re-rating — adjusted for the macro environment as of 5 June 2026, which rewards hard-dated catalysts inside the first-half-to-mid-2026 window and penalises theses that require a Federal Reserve rate cut or a confidence recovery to pay off.

Gartner has neither a hard positive catalyst nor a definable forced-selling event. What it has is a recurring quarterly data point on an unresolved question, and the macro environment's reward-and-penalty test works against it on every dimension.

Quarterly earnings are the only hard dates, but they are not decisive events. The second-quarter 2026 earnings report (roughly early August) and the third-quarter report (roughly early November) both fall inside the window the macro environment favours. But they are not consensus-shattering events — they are the same test that has failed to resolve for six straight quarters. A genuine catalyst would be a clean CV re-acceleration to 3-4% or higher, accompanied by management re-anchoring a specific numerical double-digit growth target with a date. That requires the CEO-confidence channel to ease, which the higher-for-longer, oil-shocked environment makes unlikely inside the window, and the zero-for-six forward CV guidance track record makes a favourable print a low-probability bet.

There is no corporate-action catalyst. Screening for activist, governance, or strategic-process catalysts — break-up optionality (valuing each business line separately to find a discount to a sum-of-the-parts), proxy contest, management succession, strategic review, or asset sale — turns up nothing. Management is stable. The franchise is a single coherent business, not a conglomerate with parts to separate. The capital-return programme is already at maximum aggression: the share-buyback announcement has already happened and is already reflected in the price. There is no new "initiate buyback" surprise left to deliver. The macro environment favours structural special situations in thinly covered, out-of-favour names — distressed businesses, spin-offs, forced sellers. Gartner is a heavily covered, single-franchise, large-cap quality name with none of those attributes.

The merger-and-acquisition and regulatory window offers nothing. There is no credible take-out thesis for a business with an enterprise value above $24 billion built around a single research franchise, within the relevant window.

The one event that would shatter consensus is a negative. A credible quantification of how many clients are replacing Gartner with large-language models would be an immediate re-rating lower. It has no scheduled date, management has actively avoided measuring it, and the AI-saturated market tape amplifies the downside if it ever surfaces. It is a tail risk to manage, not a catalyst to position for.

There are no other structural forcing functions — no debt maturity wall, no covenant test, no index reconstitution event.

The bottom line is the most damaging possible conclusion for an actionable trade: the bull thesis has no near-term hard catalyst that the current environment rewards, and the bear thesis has no scheduled forced-selling flush to wait for. The re-rating, in either direction, is eventless and dateless within the window that matters. That is the profile of dead near-term alpha, and the late-cycle, higher-for-longer backdrop deepens it.
valley_of_death:
The risky window — adjusted for the macro environment as of 5 June 2026.

There is no balance-sheet danger here in the traditional sense: no cash-burn hump, no debt maturity wall, no distress. Net debt of $3.25 billion against roughly $1.18 billion in annual free cash flow is comfortably serviceable at investment-grade rates, and no leverage stress appears anywhere in the available data. The consistent track record of beating quarterly earnings estimates makes an earnings-per-share shock unlikely.

The near-term danger is a time-decay trap: no positive forcing function, combined with a fundamental that keeps drifting the wrong way. The current macro environment sharpens each adverse near-term risk.

First, CV non-inflection is now the base case. The entire re-rating thesis requires the 1% first-quarter 2026 CV reading to build across the second-quarter (roughly early August) and third-quarter (roughly early November) earnings reports. Management's forward CV guidance has been wrong six times in a row, and the macro environment — consumer price index at 3.8%, oil near $92, CEO confidence still depressed — removes the demand tailwind needed for the inflection. A second sub-2% CV print compresses a stock trading at 10x free cash flow toward 8x quickly, as the expected recovery date slips another two quarters.

Second, the macro backdrop is already deteriorating further. The first quarter of 2026 flagged that "client decisions slowed somewhat in March due to geopolitics" — the very confidence channel the bull needs to recover is, on the most recent data point, still getting worse. The oil-and-inflation shock the macro environment identifies keeps the most-deferrable line item on the enterprise budget exactly where it is: deferred.

Third, the free cash flow that funds the buyback is already down 15% year over year. The buyback is the load-bearing pillar of per-share value compounding. Continued free-cash-flow erosion — plausible if CV stays near stall speed in a cost-cutting environment — slows the repurchase pace precisely when it is most needed and removes the only reliable component of the promised 12% earnings-per-share annual growth target.

Fourth, margins could step down further. Management already conceded 23.5% EBITDA margin as "the new baseline" in late 2025, a confirmed permanent reduction from the prior level. Another step-down in "a much tougher selling environment" where "the value bar is higher" would begin to migrate the pressure toward the gross margin line and validate the melting-franchise interpretation.

Fifth, the AI disclosure risk. With AI investment accounting for roughly 40% of expected 2026 earnings-per-share growth across the market, and AI the dominant market theme, any competitor announcement, client case study, or forced disclosure that puts a non-trivial number on large-language-model substitution of Gartner's product would produce an immediate, unhedgeable re-rating lower — in a tape that is maximally sensitive to exactly that question. Management has pre-committed to not measuring it.

The asymmetry is the problem: the downside catalysts — CV plateau, continued macro deterioration, a further EBITDA step-down, an AI disclosure — have hard near-term dates inside the window. The upside catalyst, CV re-acceleration, is explicitly described as a "medium term" outcome, has missed six times in a row, and requires the very macro easing the current environment denies. An investor is paid roughly a 10% free-cash-flow yield to hold through this window, but is simultaneously exposed to an undated option on a structural AI disclosure with unbounded downside.
positioning_washout:
Forced-selling pressure — adjusted for the macro environment as of 5 June 2026.

There is no mechanical forced-selling event available here. Under the macro environment's preferred investment lanes — distressed and deleveraging situations first, then spin-offs, then forced sellers — Gartner sits outside every favoured category.

Gartner pays no dividend. There is no yield-focused investor base that would be forced to sell on a dividend cut, no income-mandate liquidation event to produce a sharp, disorderly exit. The shareholder base is institutional quality-growth and GARP (growth at a reasonable price) investors, plus passive index funds as an S&P 500 constituent. That combination is unfavourable in both directions.

On the bear side: without a dividend and without debt-covenant pressure, there is no mechanism for a sharp capitulation. The stock will re-rate slowly rather than gapping down to a maximum-pessimism level — the growth-investor thesis erodes quarter by quarter as CV stays near 1%, and the holder base rotates gradually from growth toward value mandates. That is a slow grind, not a flush that creates an asymmetric entry point.

On the bull side: the same absence of forced sellers means the company itself is structurally tightening the available float. The buyback has retired roughly 8% of shares outstanding (4% in a single quarter), the investment-grade bond was issued specifically "to support even more share repurchase capacity," and insiders are buying at a purchase-to-sale ratio of 3.47. The technical setup is the opposite of a washout: a shrinking, treasury-and-insider-supported float that mechanically limits how far the stock can fall even as the fundamental drifts.

The macro environment makes this doubly disqualifying for a "wait for the wash-out and buy" approach. That approach requires a specific forced-selling event to wait for, and not only does none exist (no dividend to cut), but the corporate buyback bid actively prevents the maximum-pessimism entry level that would make the 38% gap to estimated fair value actionable. In a market environment with stretched positioning (Bank of America's Fund Manager Survey cash levels at 3.9%), there is no broad liquidity-shock mechanism visible that would produce a sharp forced-selling event either.

The one scenario that could produce meaningful selling pressure is narrative re-classification: if the market formally moves Gartner from "quality compounder" to "structurally challenged value" — a real risk given the AI-substitution question — dedicated quality-growth and quality-factor holders would rotate out. But that is a discretionary, multi-quarter rotation, not a same-day mechanical washout.

Conclusion: no forced-selling catalyst to exploit in either direction. The buyback is the dominant flow and it supports the stock rather than washing it out. The current macro environment offers no liquidity event to produce the flush.
moderator_conclusion:
The committee passes for now, with a conviction score of 2. The macro environment as of 5 June 2026 reinforces rather than rescues that decision.

Gartner is a genuinely high-quality business: 67.7% gross margin, 21% return on invested capital, free-cash-flow conversion above 100%, fifteen years of unbroken roughly 4% annual price increases. It trades at roughly 10x free cash flow with a stated 38% gap to estimated fair value, insider buying at a 3.47 purchase-to-sale ratio, and a buyback retiring roughly 8% of shares per year. Every one of those facts is corroborated and well-argued. That is precisely the trap.

If the slowdown in contract value from 8% to 1% is cyclical — DOGE-driven federal cuts that will eventually lap, a tariff shock, a record CEO-confidence freeze that eventually mean-reverts — then this is a low-double-digit-per-share compounder available at a value-trap multiple. But a sound investment framework measures the gap between reality and market perception, the timing, and the margin of safety relative to a specific catalyst that forces the re-rating. On all three of those tests, the trade fails to clear the bar, and the current macro environment pushes each one further against acting.

Where we disagree with the market is not cleanly capturable. The market is factually correct that the franchise has stalled: five-year earnings-per-share growth of 1.2%, five-year book-value-per-share growth of 0.8%, full-year 2025 net income down 39.7%, free cash flow down 15% year over year. The only open question is whether the growth rate implied by a 10x multiple is too punishing — and that question is unresolvable because management has declined for eight straight quarters to disclose any data on large-language-model substitution. An unresolvable central question is a coin toss priced roughly fairly. The macro environment's independence test makes it worse: the bull payoff depends on the same two shared macro factors that the current environment says to treat with caution — the oil-and-inflation-driven CEO-confidence channel (still adverse), and the AI-spending theme (which is the dominant market factor and the primary downside risk for this name).

The timing is asymmetric against the buyer. Downside catalysts — contract value plateauing on the next two earnings prints, a macro still deteriorating as of March 2026 under a 3.8% consumer price index, roughly $92 oil, and a Federal Reserve on hold, a further EBITDA margin step-down, and an un-hedgeable AI-substitution disclosure into an AI-saturated tape — have hard near-term dates. The upside catalyst, contract value re-acceleration, is explicitly described as a "medium term" outcome, has missed six consecutive times, and implicitly requires the very rate-and-confidence easing the Federal-Reserve-on-hold-until-December environment denies.

The catalyst that forces the re-rating is absent in both directions. Screening for activist, governance, sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately), strategic-process, or break-up catalysts returns nothing — stable management, a single coherent franchise, an already maximally aggressive capital-return programme. The macro environment favours structural special situations in thinly covered, out-of-favour names; Gartner is the opposite. And there is no mechanical forced-selling flush to wait for: no dividend to cut, and the corporate buyback bid actively prevents the maximum-pessimism entry level that would make the discount actionable.

The absence of a buy or sell signal from the screener sets the governing prior: conviction of 2 or below and a pass unless extraordinary evidence overrides it. The override test is not "is this a good business" — it is — but "does the evidence resolve the central cyclical-versus-structural question in the bull's favour, with a near-term catalyst inside the window the macro environment rewards?" It does not.

Conviction 2, rather than 1, acknowledges the real downside protection: durable free cash flow, strong cash conversion, insider and treasury buying support, and a legitimate cyclical-recovery option that keeps this from being capital-destructive even in the current environment. But the committee passes for now. The file re-opens toward a buy-now decision only on one of two extraordinary overrides: a clean contract-value re-acceleration to 3-4% or higher on the August or November 2026 earnings print, accompanied by management re-anchoring a specific numerical double-digit growth target with a date; or management finally quantifying AI substitution as immaterial with disclosed, auditable data. Until one of those arrives, the decision is pass.

bull_thesis: Gartner is an elite, capital-light subscription compounder caught at a genuine cyclical trough, on offer at ~10-12x FCF — a multiple normally reserved for melting ice cubes, applied to a franchise with 21-27% ROIC, 67.7% stable gross margins, and an 18.1% FCF margin no listed peer approaches. The core is Research (~$5.3B contract value, ~74% contribution margin), a subscription product on ~24-month contracts with >100% enterprise wallet retention sold to the most enduring buyer set in the economy — CIOs, CFOs, Chief Supply Chain Officers — whose mandate (manage AI/cyber/tariff risk, 'see around corners') survives any macro. The bull case is that the +1% Q1'26 CV print is a CYCLICAL air-pocket, not structural impairment: management names dated culprits — DOGE-driven Federal cuts, tariff/IEEPA whipsaw, deferred enterprise decisions in a 'much tougher selling environment' (Q4'25) — and ex-Federal CV is already 3.5%, well above the 1% headline. As Federal stabilizes and tariffs settle ('with 15% tariffs... we can live with that,' Q1'26), CV re-accelerates and the ~2-year subscription lag flows it into revenue through 2027. The cash engine is fully intact and corroborated: TTM FCF $1.26B (Q1'26 FCF +29% YoY), FY'26 guide FCF >=$1.16B, ~10.8% FCF yield, FCF structurally well above net income on deferred-revenue mechanics. Management converts that cash into the highest-conviction per-share lever available: $2B repurchased in 2025 (~8% share-count reduction), partly funded by an inaugural IG bond raised explicitly to expand buyback capacity, executed opportunistically INTO weakness. With revenue merely flat and the buyback shrinking the base ~8%/yr, the '12%+ adjusted EPS CAGR over the next 3 years' guide (Q1'26) is achievable largely on capital allocation alone; CV re-acceleration is upside. The reported FY'25 EPS '−40%' is an optical artifact — FY'24 carried a one-time tax benefit (+79% Q4'24 EPS); TTM EPS $10.12 and +17% latest-Q show underlying earnings stable-to-growing. The street has already capitulated: the stock cratered ~40% to a 52-wk low on the weak Feb-2026 revenue guide, targets were cut to ~$199 (from ~$253), price sits 9% off the low with −20% momentum vs 200d SMA — a fully-washed-out quality name. Conditions: CV troughs in 1H-2026 and inflects up; AskGartner/the 'four-dimension transformation' defends renewal vs general-purpose LLMs; buyback continues at scale with prudent leverage.
bear_thesis: Gartner is cheap for a reason: this is a research-CONTENT business whose core product — curated, trusted, written human insight — is exactly the category general-purpose AI is built to commoditize, and the +1% Q1'26 CV print (from +8% a year earlier) may be the first quantitative tremor of structural impairment, not a cyclical dip. interrogator_dossier:
# Gartner, Inc. (IT) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator: Claude Opus 4.8 · Coverage: Q2-2024 → Q1-2026 transcripts cross-referenced against screener financials · Classification: deep_value (Industrials/B2B research & advisory)*

Management's words are the hypothesis; the trajectory is the evidence. Seven consecutive quarterly calls cross-referenced against the screener's hard financials.

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Across these seven quarters Gartner's story does not pivot so much as **erode in slow motion under a constant veneer of confidence**. The opening frame (Q2'24) is unmistakably *resilience-into-reacceleration*: Safian declares "we believe the first quarter [2024] marked the bottom for CV growth this cycle," management reiterates the long-run promise of "sustained double-digit revenue growth," and the headline number — contract value (CV) — is accelerating, +7% in Q2'24, +7% Q3'24, +8% Q4'24. The growth drivers emphasized early are concrete and operational: tech-vendor CV "turning the corner" after the small-tech-company funding washout, GTS/GBS both guided to "12% to 16% medium term," and an accelerating sales-headcount build ("the largest sequential increase since Q4 of 2022") framed explicitly as a 2025-26-27 productivity investment.

By Q4'25 (Feb-2026) the arc has visibly bent. The double-digit CV promise is gone from the headline; CV growth has decelerated and management introduces an entirely **new, abstract narrative scaffold** — the "transformation of business and technology insights along four dimensions: impact, volume, timeliness, user experience." This is the tell. When a subscription compounder stops leading with CV acceleration and starts leading with a qualitative four-pillar transformation framework and a list of external villains ("DOGE-related initiatives… evolving trade policies… funding changes… country-specific factors"), the growth engine is sputtering and the story is being re-based around *process* rather than *output*.

By Q1'26 (May-2026) the deceleration is severe and undeniable: **CV growth has collapsed to +1% year-over-year** (3.5% ex-U.S.-Federal), the worst print in the series by a wide margin, versus +8% just a year prior. Management reframes again — now the durable assets are "enduring roles" (CIO, CSCO, CFO) and "trust is at a premium" in an information-overload / AI world, and the forward promise is narrowed and quantified to "adjusted EPS on a compound annual basis above 12% over the next 3 years." Note the substitution: the long-running *revenue* promise ("sustained double-digit revenue growth") has been quietly retired and replaced by an *EPS* promise — a number buybacks alone (8% share shrink in 2025) can largely manufacture even with revenue flat. That is the single most important narrative shift in the entire arc.

A genuine structural event also lands inside the window: in Q4'25 management announces "a definitive agreement to sell our digital markets business," and by Q1'26 the company speaks of results "excluding the divested operation" — i.e., the sale has closed. It is presented as one of several "shareholder value-enhancing actions" alongside the $2B buyback and an inaugural investment-grade bond. It is portfolio cleanup, not a thesis (the divested unit — Capterra/Software Advice/GetApp — fetched only ~$110M).

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim (quarter) | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| "Sustained double-digit revenue growth" (long-run, every call Q2'24–Q4'24) | Rev: 5.91B('23)→6.27B('24, **+6%**)→6.50B('25, **+3.7%**). 3-yr CAGR **+5.9%**. Q1'26 revenue **−1% FX-neutral.** | **Diverges hard.** The "double-digit" promise was never in the actual revenue line and is now contradicted outright — revenue is decelerating toward zero. Aspirational TAM-math, not trajectory. |
| "Q1'24 marked the bottom for CV growth" (Q2'24) | CV growth 7%→7%→8% (good) then **rolled over to +1% by Q1'26.** | **False in the medium term.** A local bottom, not the cycle bottom. CV re-decelerated to a series low. Credibility cost is high — it was stated as near-certainty. |
| "Modest margin expansion over time" (every call) | Net margin **11.2%** ('25) on EPS that fell **−39.7% YoY** ('25); mgmt concedes Q4'25 **23.5% EBITDA margin is "the new baseline"** — a *step-DOWN* from ~25%. | **Diverges.** "Expansion over time" is hedged, but the realized move was contraction and a guided-down baseline. Story richer than the P&L. |
| "Significant free cash flow well in excess of net income" (every call) | TTM FCF **1.26B** vs TTM NI ~0.68B; FCF margin **18.1%**; Q1'26 FCF **+29% YoY**. FY'26 guide FCF ≥**1.16B**. | **TRUE and corroborated.** Deferred-revenue subscription mechanics make FCF structurally exceed NI. The one big claim the numbers fully support. |
| "Reduced shares 8% in 2025" / "return capital" (Q4'25) | $2B repurchased 2025; bvps_5y CAGR **+0.8%**, eps_5y **+1.2%** — EPS growth has come almost *entirely* from share-count shrink, not earnings growth. | **TRUE but double-edged.** Buyback is real and aggressive, but is now the *primary* EPS lever masking a stalled top line. ROE 203.7% is a buyback/leverage artifact, not operating quality. |
| ROIC / returns excellence (implied throughout) | ROIC **21–27%** (Q1'26 rolling 27%); gross margin **67.7% stable**. | **TRUE.** The capital-light subscription moat is real; return profile genuinely elite. Not in dispute. |
| New "12%+ EPS 3-yr CAGR" promise (Q1'26) | FY'24 EPS 16.0 → FY'25 EPS **9.65 (−39.7%)**, but '24 was inflated by tax-planning ("benefit… from our tax planning initiatives," +79% Q4'24 EPS). TTM EPS **10.12**, latest-Q **+17%**. | **Mixed.** The −40% EPS "collapse" is largely an optical artifact of a 2024 one-time tax benefit unwinding, NOT operational implosion — TTM 10.12 and +17% latest-Q confirm underlying earnings stable-to-growing. The 12% forward CAGR is plausible *because of buybacks*, not revenue. |

**Net:** the forensic gap is concentrated in one place — the **CV/revenue growth narrative is materially richer than the trajectory justifies**, while the headline EPS "−40%" scare is *poorer* than reality justifies (tax distortion). Cash-generation and returns claims are fully real.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING (offensive → defensive), masked by relentlessly upbeat scripting.**

- **Q2'24 — Offensive.** "We believe the first quarter marked the bottom for CV growth this cycle." Specific, forward, growth-framed.
- **Q3'24 — Confident.** "Tech vendor CV growth has turned the corner and continues to accelerate." Raised guidance.
- **Q4'24 — Peak confidence.** "CV growth accelerated to almost 8%"; 2025 guide "achievable with opportunity for upside"; Q4 EPS +79% (tax-aided). High-water mark.
- **Q4'25 — Defensive pivot.** Language inverts: "2025 was a unique year due to a range of external market forces… DOGE… trade policies." "Executives have responded… by slowing and deferring everything possible. This makes for a much tougher selling environment." The four-dimension "transformation" arrives — a classic redirection from missed output to internal process. Margin baseline guided *down*. Still insists CV will "accelerate in 2026."
- **Q1'26 — Defensive, philosophical, hedged.** "Client decisions slowed somewhat in March." Heavy emphasis on intangibles — "trust is at a premium," "enduring roles," "see around corners." Forward promise narrows from revenue to a buyback-aided **EPS** CAGR. The tell: when pressed on sub-$50K-ASP discounting, Safian is emphatic — "I could assure you we are not… there's no change in our discounting posture." Defending price integrity unprompted signals the street suspects demand-driven concessions.

The signature is the move from **specificity to abstraction**: early calls quantify CV by channel and reaffirm 12-16% targets; late calls retreat to four-pillar frameworks and "enduring roles." Hall's register is identically "incredibly optimistic" in Q1'26 as in Q2'24 — which is itself a yellow flag, because the financials underneath moved a great deal and the tone did not move at all.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Score: MEDIUM.** A genuine split-personality record.

- **The good (corroborates the 7/7 beat rate):** beat 7/7 quarters (100%); every call says results "ahead of expectations," guidance set "achievable with opportunity for upside" (Q4'24), and they *raised* EBITDA/EPS/FCF guidance in Q1'26 even as CV crawled. On metrics they fully control — FCF, EBITDA conversion, buyback execution — reliability is **High**.
- **The bad (the load-bearing forecast they whiffed):** the prediction that mattered most for the long thesis — "Q1'24 marked the bottom for CV growth this cycle" + the standing "12-16% medium-term" target — was **wrong by a mile**; CV decelerated to +1%. On the metric that actually drives intrinsic value (CV → forward subscription revenue), reliability is **Low**.

Reconciliation: Gartner sandbags the *near-term P&L* it controls and over-promises the *medium-term growth* it does not. Trust the FCF guide; distrust "CV will accelerate in 2026" until the print confirms (Q1'26 "+1%, accelerating" is technically an acceleration off Q4'25 but off a near-zero base — a low bar dressed as momentum).

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **Retention erosion — recurring, every call.** Silber (BMO) pushes Q2'24 ("the retention metrics still going down"); persists into Q1'26 (client retention ticking down while wallet retention ticks up). Management attributes it to "small tech vendor churn" and, by Q1'26, to clients cutting *seats* ("I've got 10 seats, one of them… cut that seat out"). Enterprise wallet retention is fine; the drag is real but narrated as transient for two years running.
- **AI as a substitution threat — the buried landmine.** Sabadra (RBC) asks directly (Q4'24): "how often does LLM… come up… as an alternative to Gartner?" Hall waves it off — "a very, very, very small number of transactions." This is the existential question for a *research-content* business in 2026, answered with a flat denial and no supporting metric. By Q1'26 Hall has flipped the frame — Gartner is now "the best source for clients looking to achieve success on their AI journeys" and "world-class users of AI internally," repositioning the disruptor as a demand driver. The topic most likely to "later turn into bad news."
- **Margin step-down — Q4'25/Q1'26.** Goldman's proxy: "Your margin guidance suggests a step down… Is this the new baseline?" Safian concedes 23.5% EBITDA is "the new baseline." Analysts now model structurally lower margins.
- **Discounting pressure — Q1'26.** Silber relays "speculation… sales teams have made offers… below the normal $50,000 ASP." Management's emphatic denial is the point of maximum defensiveness in the series.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

**🔴 Red flags**
- 🔴 **CV growth collapse 8% → 1%** (Q4'24 → Q1'26) — the lead operating metric for a subscription model in freefall, after management called the bottom 18 months early.
- 🔴 **Revenue promise downgraded and swapped for a buyback-aided EPS promise** — "double-digit revenue growth" retired, replaced by "12%+ EPS CAGR" share-shrink can fabricate on flat revenue (eps_5y CAGR only +1.2%).
- 🔴 **Margin baseline guided DOWN** to 23.5% EBITDA from ~25%.
- 🔴 **AI substitution risk dismissed without data** — the central terminal-value question for a research-content franchise, answered with rhetoric.
- 🔴 **Unprompted defense of pricing integrity** (Q1'26) — the street smells demand-driven concessions.
- 🔴 **Levering up to fund buyback** — inaugural IG bond explicitly to expand repurchase capacity; financial engineering doing the heavy lifting. *(NB: scan shows net debt 3.25B; live correction recomputes 1.59B NET-CASH at 2026-03-31 — the two conflict; reconcile before sizing. Either way the IG-bond-for-buyback posture is real.)*

**🟢 Green flags**
- 🟢 **Elite, durable returns** — ROIC 21–27%, gross margin 67.7% stable, FCF margin 18.1%. Capital-light subscription moat intact, superior to any listed peer.
- 🟢 **FCF "well in excess of net income," fully delivered** — TTM FCF 1.26B, Q1'26 FCF +29% YoY; ~10.8% FCF yield at the current price.
- 🟢 **Disciplined, opportunistic buyback** — $2B in 2025, ~8% share reduction, executed into weakness. Per-share compounding lever is real.
- 🟢 **100% beat rate (7/7) and credible near-term P&L sandbagging** — raised EBITDA/EPS/FCF guide in Q1'26.
- 🟢 **Genuinely enduring demand substrate** — >100% enterprise wallet retention (ex-small-tech) is real; "enduring roles" framing is not just spin.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The metric substitution is the loudest signal:** management re-anchored the long-term promise from *revenue* to *EPS* — the language of a company that knows the top line is structurally slower and is steering investors toward the lever (buyback) it can still pull.
- **"Transformation along four dimensions"** appears nowhere before Q4'25 and dominates Q4'25–Q1'26. A new, abstract, output-free framework introduced exactly as the headline metric rolls over is almost always a narrative bridge over a growth air-pocket.
- **Competitive framing on AI inverted** within two quarters — from "clients almost never cite LLMs as an alternative" (denial, Q4'24) to "no one is better positioned to guide leaders on their AI journeys" (co-option, Q4'25–Q1'26). Watch whether AskGartner (rolled out Aug–Oct 2025, claimed to lift renewal rates) is a genuine moat-deepener or a defensive necessity against LLM commoditization.
- **Capital-allocation tone shifted from "return capital" to "increase leverage to return MORE capital."** Levering a slowing-growth compounder to buy stock at a trough multiple is shareholder-friendly *if* the multiple is genuinely depressed — value-destroying if growth keeps fading. The bet is explicit.
- **The divestiture is tiny and telling:** ~$110M for Digital Markets — a rounding error vs ~$11B market cap. Its prominence in the Q4'25 "shareholder value-enhancing actions" list shows how little hard catalyst there is to point to.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: DETERIORATING (operating growth) wrapped in a STABLE, elite cash-return machine.**

This is not a broken business and not a balance-sheet value trap — it is a **quality compounder whose growth engine has stalled to ~1% CV while management buys time with an abstract transformation narrative and an aggressive, leveraged buyback that converts a flat top line into a low-double-digit EPS CAGR.** The moat (renewal-based subscription research, 21-27% ROIC, 67% gross margin, ~11% FCF yield) is real and durable; the growth re-acceleration management keeps promising has now been wrong for six straight quarters.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** *Is the CV deceleration to +1% a cyclical, macro-driven air-pocket (tariffs / DOGE / deferred enterprise decisions) that re-accelerates as management insists — or the early signal of structural impairment as general-purpose AI commoditizes the "trusted research content" that is Gartner's core product?* The entire intrinsic-value case rests on that fork. If cyclical, ~10-12x FCF with a buyback shrinking the share count 8%/yr is a genuinely cheap compounder bought at a trough. If structural (AI substitution), the multiple is cheap *for a reason* and the buyback is levering into a fade. Management's flat, metric-free denial of the AI threat is the most important unanswered question in this file. Critically, **there is no hard-dated structural catalyst to force resolution** — the Digital Markets sale already closed and was immaterial — so a director is paid only the FCF yield and the buyback while waiting for CV prints to adjudicate the fork.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: DETERIORATING

bull_thesis: Gartner is an elite, capital-light subscription compounder caught at a genuine cyclical trough, on offer at ~10-12x FCF — a multiple normally reserved for melting ice cubes, applied to a franchise with 21-27% ROIC, 67.7% stable gross margins, and an 18.1% FCF margin no listed peer approaches. The core is Research (~$5.3B contract value, ~74% contribution margin), a subscription product on ~24-month contracts with >100% enterprise wallet retention sold to the most enduring buyer set in the economy — CIOs, CFOs, Chief Supply Chain Officers — whose mandate (manage AI/cyber/tariff risk, 'see around corners') survives any macro. The bull case is that the +1% Q1'26 CV print is a CYCLICAL air-pocket, not structural impairment: management names dated culprits — DOGE-driven Federal cuts, tariff/IEEPA whipsaw, deferred enterprise decisions in a 'much tougher selling environment' (Q4'25) — and ex-Federal CV is already 3.5%, well above the 1% headline. As Federal stabilizes and tariffs settle ('with 15% tariffs... we can live with that,' Q1'26), CV re-accelerates and the ~2-year subscription lag flows it into revenue through 2027. The cash engine is fully intact and corroborated: TTM FCF $1.26B (Q1'26 FCF +29% YoY), FY'26 guide FCF >=$1.16B, ~10.8% FCF yield, FCF structurally well above net income on deferred-revenue mechanics. Management converts that cash into the highest-conviction per-share lever available: $2B repurchased in 2025 (~8% share-count reduction), partly funded by an inaugural IG bond raised explicitly to expand buyback capacity, executed opportunistically INTO weakness. bear_thesis: Gartner is cheap for a reason: this is a research-CONTENT business whose core product — curated, trusted, written human insight — is exactly the category general-purpose AI is built to commoditize, and the +1% Q1'26 CV print (from +8% a year earlier) may be the first quantitative tremor of structural impairment, not a cyclical dip. consensus_delta: Deep-value framing: is the street pricing terminal decline into a company with stable/growing earnings power? Partially. interrogator_dossier:
# Gartner, Inc. (IT) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator: Claude Opus 4.8 · Coverage: Q2-2024 → Q1-2026 transcripts cross-referenced against screener financials · Classification: deep_value (Industrials/B2B research & advisory)*

Management's words are the hypothesis; the trajectory is the evidence. Seven consecutive quarterly calls cross-referenced against the screener's hard financials.

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Across these seven quarters Gartner's story does not pivot so much as **erode in slow motion under a constant veneer of confidence**. The opening frame (Q2'24) is unmistakably *resilience-into-reacceleration*: Safian declares "we believe the first quarter [2024] marked the bottom for CV growth this cycle," management reiterates the long-run promise of "sustained double-digit revenue growth," and the headline number — contract value (CV) — is accelerating, +7% in Q2'24, +7% Q3'24, +8% Q4'24. The growth drivers emphasized early are concrete and operational: tech-vendor CV "turning the corner" after the small-tech-company funding washout, GTS/GBS both guided to "12% to 16% medium term," and an accelerating sales-headcount build ("the largest sequential increase since Q4 of 2022") framed explicitly as a 2025-26-27 productivity investment.

By Q4'25 (Feb-2026) the arc has visibly bent. The double-digit CV promise is gone from the headline; CV growth has decelerated and management introduces an entirely **new, abstract narrative scaffold** — the "transformation of business and technology insights along four dimensions: impact, volume, timeliness, user experience." This is the tell. When a subscription compounder stops leading with CV acceleration and starts leading with a qualitative four-pillar transformation framework and a list of external villains ("DOGE-related initiatives… evolving trade policies… funding changes… country-specific factors"), the growth engine is sputtering and the story is being re-based around *process* rather than *output*.

By Q1'26 (May-2026) the deceleration is severe and undeniable: **CV growth has collapsed to +1% year-over-year** (3.5% ex-U.S.-Federal), the worst print in the series by a wide margin, versus +8% just a year prior. Management reframes again — now the durable assets are "enduring roles" (CIO, CSCO, CFO) and "trust is at a premium" in an information-overload / AI world, and the forward promise is narrowed and quantified to "adjusted EPS on a compound annual basis above 12% over the next 3 years." Note the substitution: the long-running *revenue* promise ("sustained double-digit revenue growth") has been quietly retired and replaced by an *EPS* promise — a number buybacks alone (8% share shrink in 2025) can largely manufacture even with revenue flat. That is the single most important narrative shift in the entire arc.

A genuine structural event also lands inside the window: in Q4'25 management announces "a definitive agreement to sell our digital markets business," and by Q1'26 the company speaks of results "excluding the divested operation" — i.e., the sale has closed. It is presented as one of several "shareholder value-enhancing actions" alongside the $2B buyback and an inaugural investment-grade bond. It is portfolio cleanup, not a thesis (the divested unit — Capterra/Software Advice/GetApp — fetched only ~$110M).

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim (quarter) | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| "Sustained double-digit revenue growth" (long-run, every call Q2'24–Q4'24) | Rev: 5.91B('23)→6.27B('24, **+6%**)→6.50B('25, **+3.7%**). 3-yr CAGR **+5.9%**. Q1'26 revenue **−1% FX-neutral.** | **Diverges hard.** The "double-digit" promise was never in the actual revenue line and is now contradicted outright — revenue is decelerating toward zero. Aspirational TAM-math, not trajectory. |
| "Q1'24 marked the bottom for CV growth" (Q2'24) | CV growth 7%→7%→8% (good) then **rolled over to +1% by Q1'26.** | **False in the medium term.** A local bottom, not the cycle bottom. CV re-decelerated to a series low. Credibility cost is high — it was stated as near-certainty. |
| "Modest margin expansion over time" (every call) | Net margin **11.2%** ('25) on EPS that fell **−39.7% YoY** ('25); mgmt concedes Q4'25 **23.5% EBITDA margin is "the new baseline"** — a *step-DOWN* from ~25%. | **Diverges.** "Expansion over time" is hedged, but the realized move was contraction and a guided-down baseline. Story richer than the P&L. |
| "Significant free cash flow well in excess of net income" (every call) | TTM FCF **1.26B** vs TTM NI ~0.68B; FCF margin **18.1%**; Q1'26 FCF **+29% YoY**. FY'26 guide FCF ≥**1.16B**. | **TRUE and corroborated.** Deferred-revenue subscription mechanics make FCF structurally exceed NI. The one big claim the numbers fully support. |
| "Reduced shares 8% in 2025" / "return capital" (Q4'25) | $2B repurchased 2025; bvps_5y CAGR **+0.8%**, eps_5y **+1.2%** — EPS growth has come almost *entirely* from share-count shrink, not earnings growth. | **TRUE but double-edged.** Buyback is real and aggressive, but is now the *primary* EPS lever masking a stalled top line. ROE 203.7% is a buyback/leverage artifact, not operating quality. |
| ROIC / returns excellence (implied throughout) | ROIC **21–27%** (Q1'26 rolling 27%); gross margin **67.7% stable**. | **TRUE.** The capital-light subscription moat is real; return profile genuinely elite. Not in dispute. |
| New "12%+ EPS 3-yr CAGR" promise (Q1'26) | FY'24 EPS 16.0 → FY'25 EPS **9.65 (−39.7%)**, but '24 was inflated by tax-planning ("benefit… from our tax planning initiatives," +79% Q4'24 EPS). TTM EPS **10.12**, latest-Q **+17%**. | **Mixed.** The −40% EPS "collapse" is largely an optical artifact of a 2024 one-time tax benefit unwinding, NOT operational implosion — TTM 10.12 and +17% latest-Q confirm underlying earnings stable-to-growing. The 12% forward CAGR is plausible *because of buybacks*, not revenue. |

**Net:** the forensic gap is concentrated in one place — the **CV/revenue growth narrative is materially richer than the trajectory justifies**, while the headline EPS "−40%" scare is *poorer* than reality justifies (tax distortion). Cash-generation and returns claims are fully real.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING (offensive → defensive), masked by relentlessly upbeat scripting.**

- **Q2'24 — Offensive.** "We believe the first quarter marked the bottom for CV growth this cycle." Specific, forward, growth-framed.
- **Q3'24 — Confident.** "Tech vendor CV growth has turned the corner and continues to accelerate." Raised guidance.
- **Q4'24 — Peak confidence.** "CV growth accelerated to almost 8%"; 2025 guide "achievable with opportunity for upside"; Q4 EPS +79% (tax-aided). High-water mark.
- **Q4'25 — Defensive pivot.** Language inverts: "2025 was a unique year due to a range of external market forces… DOGE… trade policies." "Executives have responded… by slowing and deferring everything possible. This makes for a much tougher selling environment." The four-dimension "transformation" arrives — a classic redirection from missed output to internal process. Margin baseline guided *down*. Still insists CV will "accelerate in 2026."
- **Q1'26 — Defensive, philosophical, hedged.** "Client decisions slowed somewhat in March." Heavy emphasis on intangibles — "trust is at a premium," "enduring roles," "see around corners." Forward promise narrows from revenue to a buyback-aided **EPS** CAGR. The tell: when pressed on sub-$50K-ASP discounting, Safian is emphatic — "I could assure you we are not… there's no change in our discounting posture." Defending price integrity unprompted signals the street suspects demand-driven concessions.

The signature is the move from **specificity to abstraction**: early calls quantify CV by channel and reaffirm 12-16% targets; late calls retreat to four-pillar frameworks and "enduring roles." Hall's register is identically "incredibly optimistic" in Q1'26 as in Q2'24 — which is itself a yellow flag, because the financials underneath moved a great deal and the tone did not move at all.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Score: MEDIUM.** A genuine split-personality record.

- **The good (corroborates the 7/7 beat rate):** beat 7/7 quarters (100%); every call says results "ahead of expectations," guidance set "achievable with opportunity for upside" (Q4'24), and they *raised* EBITDA/EPS/FCF guidance in Q1'26 even as CV crawled. On metrics they fully control — FCF, EBITDA conversion, buyback execution — reliability is **High**.
- **The bad (the load-bearing forecast they whiffed):** the prediction that mattered most for the long thesis — "Q1'24 marked the bottom for CV growth this cycle" + the standing "12-16% medium-term" target — was **wrong by a mile**; CV decelerated to +1%. On the metric that actually drives intrinsic value (CV → forward subscription revenue), reliability is **Low**.

Reconciliation: Gartner sandbags the *near-term P&L* it controls and over-promises the *medium-term growth* it does not. Trust the FCF guide; distrust "CV will accelerate in 2026" until the print confirms (Q1'26 "+1%, accelerating" is technically an acceleration off Q4'25 but off a near-zero base — a low bar dressed as momentum).

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **Retention erosion — recurring, every call.** Silber (BMO) pushes Q2'24 ("the retention metrics still going down"); persists into Q1'26 (client retention ticking down while wallet retention ticks up). Management attributes it to "small tech vendor churn" and, by Q1'26, to clients cutting *seats* ("I've got 10 seats, one of them… cut that seat out"). Enterprise wallet retention is fine; the drag is real but narrated as transient for two years running.
- **AI as a substitution threat — the buried landmine.** Sabadra (RBC) asks directly (Q4'24): "how often does LLM… come up… as an alternative to Gartner?" Hall waves it off — "a very, very, very small number of transactions." This is the existential question for a *research-content* business in 2026, answered with a flat denial and no supporting metric. By Q1'26 Hall has flipped the frame — Gartner is now "the best source for clients looking to achieve success on their AI journeys" and "world-class users of AI internally," repositioning the disruptor as a demand driver. The topic most likely to "later turn into bad news."
- **Margin step-down — Q4'25/Q1'26.** Goldman's proxy: "Your margin guidance suggests a step down… Is this the new baseline?" Safian concedes 23.5% EBITDA is "the new baseline." Analysts now model structurally lower margins.
- **Discounting pressure — Q1'26.** Silber relays "speculation… sales teams have made offers… below the normal $50,000 ASP." Management's emphatic denial is the point of maximum defensiveness in the series.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

**🔴 Red flags**
- 🔴 **CV growth collapse 8% → 1%** (Q4'24 → Q1'26) — the lead operating metric for a subscription model in freefall, after management called the bottom 18 months early.
- 🔴 **Revenue promise downgraded and swapped for a buyback-aided EPS promise** — "double-digit revenue growth" retired, replaced by "12%+ EPS CAGR" share-shrink can fabricate on flat revenue (eps_5y CAGR only +1.2%).
- 🔴 **Margin baseline guided DOWN** to 23.5% EBITDA from ~25%.
- 🔴 **AI substitution risk dismissed without data** — the central terminal-value question for a research-content franchise, answered with rhetoric.
- 🔴 **Unprompted defense of pricing integrity** (Q1'26) — the street smells demand-driven concessions.
- 🔴 **Levering up to fund buyback** — inaugural IG bond explicitly to expand repurchase capacity; financial engineering doing the heavy lifting. *(NB: scan shows net debt 3.25B; live correction recomputes 1.59B NET-CASH at 2026-03-31 — the two conflict; reconcile before sizing. Either way the IG-bond-for-buyback posture is real.)*

**🟢 Green flags**
- 🟢 **Elite, durable returns** — ROIC 21–27%, gross margin 67.7% stable, FCF margin 18.1%. Capital-light subscription moat intact, superior to any listed peer.
- 🟢 **FCF "well in excess of net income," fully delivered** — TTM FCF 1.26B, Q1'26 FCF +29% YoY; ~10.8% FCF yield at the current price.
- 🟢 **Disciplined, opportunistic buyback** — $2B in 2025, ~8% share reduction, executed into weakness. Per-share compounding lever is real.
- 🟢 **100% beat rate (7/7) and credible near-term P&L sandbagging** — raised EBITDA/EPS/FCF guide in Q1'26.
- 🟢 **Genuinely enduring demand substrate** — >100% enterprise wallet retention (ex-small-tech) is real; "enduring roles" framing is not just spin.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The metric substitution is the loudest signal:** management re-anchored the long-term promise from *revenue* to *EPS* — the language of a company that knows the top line is structurally slower and is steering investors toward the lever (buyback) it can still pull.
- **"Transformation along four dimensions"** appears nowhere before Q4'25 and dominates Q4'25–Q1'26. A new, abstract, output-free framework introduced exactly as the headline metric rolls over is almost always a narrative bridge over a growth air-pocket.
- **Competitive framing on AI inverted** within two quarters — from "clients almost never cite LLMs as an alternative" (denial, Q4'24) to "no one is better positioned to guide leaders on their AI journeys" (co-option, Q4'25–Q1'26). Watch whether AskGartner (rolled out Aug–Oct 2025, claimed to lift renewal rates) is a genuine moat-deepener or a defensive necessity against LLM commoditization.
- **Capital-allocation tone shifted from "return capital" to "increase leverage to return MORE capital."** Levering a slowing-growth compounder to buy stock at a trough multiple is shareholder-friendly *if* the multiple is genuinely depressed — value-destroying if growth keeps fading. # Forensic Dossier — Gartner, Inc. (IT)

*Note on identity: the screener tags this "Industrials" with an "Events 54% / Consulting 46%" segment block. That is a data artifact — those are the small non-subscription pieces. IT is **Gartner, Inc.**, a technology research & advisory subscription business whose engine is the **Insights** (formerly Research) segment — ~$5B of recurring contract value at ~77% contribution margin. The dossier and SoP treat it as the subscription-research compounder it actually is.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Across the six consecutive calls (Q1-25 through Q1-26), the story moves through three distinct chapters, and management is remarkably consistent — almost scripted — in how it frames each.

**Chapter 1 — "Resilient in a complex environment" (Q1-25, Q2-25).** The opening posture is defensive-but-confident. Q1-25: *"Gartner remains resilient in a complex environment… contract value grew 7%."* The thesis is that the franchise is durable through volatility ("COVID… tech bubble… highest inflation in 40 years… first ground war in Europe in 80 years"), and the new headwind — *"government policy and tariff changes"* — is just the latest in a series the moat absorbs. By Q2-25 the tone hardens defensively: CEO confidence *"fell to recessionary levels, among the fastest drops ever recorded,"* DOGE is named as *"the largest headwind in Q2,"* and tariffs push *"purchase decisions… escalated to the CFO or even the CEO."* The growth algorithm (12–16% CV target) is explicitly deferred to *"when the macroeconomic environment returns to normal."*

**Chapter 2 — "Adaptations / path back to double-digit" (Q2-25 → Q4-25).** A pivot from defense to a self-help / transformation narrative. AskGartner (an AI tool *"in development for almost 2 years"*) is rolled out; Research is renamed **Business and Technology Insights**; and Q4-25 introduces a structured *"transformation across business and technology insights along 4 dimensions: impact, volume, timeliness, user experience."* This is the classic playbook of a high-quality compounder whose growth has stalled cyclically: reframe the stall as a deliberate investment phase, give it a memorable four-pillar structure, and put a clock on it ("over the next few years").

**Chapter 3 — "Acceleration is here" (Q4-25, Q1-26).** The forward language turns offensive again. Q1-26: *"Year-over-year contract value growth accelerated in the first quarter"* and the punchline guidance — *"we expect to deliver adjusted EPS on a compound annual basis above 12% over the next 3 years."* The federal headwind is explicitly being lapped (US Fed CV down to ~$114M / ~4% of total). The AI narrative flips from threat-to-defend ("is an LLM replacing Gartner?") to opportunity-to-capture ("highest demand topic," 6,000+ AI documents, AskGartner lifting renewal rates).

**Drivers quietly dropped / added:** the "small tech vendor churn" drag on enterprise count is mentioned every quarter but consistently waved off as low-dollar. The federal business shrank from a renewable base to a ~50%-retention rump and was effectively written down in the narrative. The genuinely *new* driver introduced is the AI-internal-productivity story (content per analyst +31% YoY, publishing time -75%) — a margin and value lever, not yet a revenue one.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Free cash flow well in excess of net income" | every call | TTM FCF $1.26B vs TTM diluted EPS $10.12 (NI ~$0.78B FY25); FCF margin 18.1% | **TRUE & load-bearing.** FCF/NI > 1.6x. This is the real story the EPS line hides. |

Adjusted EPS is *growing double digits*. The peer-comp note's "likely a non-cash charge" instinct is correct in direction. |

The acceleration is real but early and small. |

| ROIC / capital efficiency | Q1-26 | ROIC 21% (screener) / **~27% rolling 4-qtr (mgmt)**; ROE 203.7% | **TRUE & elite.** Asset-light subscription model; ROE is inflated by the buyback-driven thin equity base but ROIC ~21–27% is genuinely high-quality. |

**Where the story is richer than the numbers:** the "clear path back to double-digit growth" has been promised for four straight quarters while CV decelerated from +7% to +1%. **Where the story is poorer than the numbers:** management *under*-sells the FCF and buyback story. At 9x P/FCF with 8% annual share shrink, the per-share FCF compounding is more powerful than the muted CV-growth narrative conveys. This is the core of the deep-value thesis.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: Declining-then-Stabilizing (a "V" — bottomed Q2/Q3-25, recovering into Q1-26).**

- **Q1-25 (Stable-confident):** *"resilient… ahead of our expectations… eager to repurchase shares aggressively."* Offensive.

- **Q3-25 (Stabilizing):** *"adaptations that are starting to yield results… contract renewal rates improved from the second quarter… repurchased more than $1 billion."* First green shoots language.
- **Q4-25 (Cautiously offensive):** *"well positioned to accelerate CV growth throughout 2026,"* but margin guide steps *down* to 23.5% — they are choosing to invest, signaling confidence in the runway, not the quarter.
- **Q1-26 (Offensive again, with a hedge):** *"contract value growth accelerated… EPS CAGR above 12% over the next 3 years… teams are incredibly optimistic"* — but immediately hedged: *"client decisions slowed somewhat in March"* due to geopolitics, and 2026 selling environment assumed *"no better… than 2025."*

**Reliability: HIGH.** This is the strongest pillar of the dossier.

- The pattern is textbook **sandbagging**: management openly tells you they don't *"build forecasts… based on what we hope is going to happen, but more along what we've seen"* (Q1-25, on federal renewals) — they deliberately exclude upside (e.g. federal clients returning in '26/'27) from guidance. A 7/7 beat rate with explicit conservatism is the signature of a low-ball-and-deliver operator.
- The one credibility watch-item: the *"path back to double-digit CV"* has been a moving target for a year. They have delivered on EPS/FCF/margin guidance every time, but the CV-reacceleration *timing* is the claim most exposed to a future miss. To date, however, sequential CV has in fact inflected up (Q4-25 low → Q1-26 +1% / +3.5% ex-Fed), so even this is tracking.

Net: management says less than it could and delivers more than it says. Score **HIGH**.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"Is an LLM replacing the Gartner subscription?"** — recurring across Q2-25, Q3-25, Q1-26 (Jason Haas, Ashish Sabadra). Management's answer is consistent and, crucially, *data-backed*: they track every cancellation reason and LLM-substitution is *"essentially unmeasurable… a very, very, very small number of transactions."* This is the bear case's load-bearing fear and management meets it with the one thing that rebuts it — loss-reason tracking. No topic that analysts pushed on later turned into a *negative surprise* in this window — a clean record.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **CV growth decelerated to +1% total (Q1-26)** from +7% (Q1-25) — the franchise *did* stall, and "acceleration" off a +1% base is a low bar; the 12–16% target remains aspirational.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS
