# Forensic Dossier — Gartner, Inc. (IT)

*Note on identity: the screener tags this "Industrials" with an "Events 54% / Consulting 46%" segment block. That is a data artifact — those are the small non-subscription pieces. IT is **Gartner, Inc.**, a technology research & advisory subscription business whose engine is the **Insights** (formerly Research) segment — ~$5B of recurring contract value at ~77% contribution margin. The dossier and SoP treat it as the subscription-research compounder it actually is.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Across the six consecutive calls (Q1-25 through Q1-26), the story moves through three distinct chapters, and management is remarkably consistent — almost scripted — in how it frames each.

**Chapter 1 — "Resilient in a complex environment" (Q1-25, Q2-25).** The opening posture is defensive-but-confident. Q1-25: *"Gartner remains resilient in a complex environment… contract value grew 7%."* The thesis is that the franchise is durable through volatility ("COVID… tech bubble… highest inflation in 40 years… first ground war in Europe in 80 years"), and the new headwind — *"government policy and tariff changes"* — is just the latest in a series the moat absorbs. By Q2-25 the tone hardens defensively: CEO confidence *"fell to recessionary levels, among the fastest drops ever recorded,"* DOGE is named as *"the largest headwind in Q2,"* and tariffs push *"purchase decisions… escalated to the CFO or even the CEO."* The growth algorithm (12–16% CV target) is explicitly deferred to *"when the macroeconomic environment returns to normal."*

**Chapter 2 — "Adaptations / path back to double-digit" (Q2-25 → Q4-25).** A pivot from defense to a self-help / transformation narrative. AskGartner (an AI tool *"in development for almost 2 years"*) is rolled out; Research is renamed **Business and Technology Insights**; and Q4-25 introduces a structured *"transformation across business and technology insights along 4 dimensions: impact, volume, timeliness, user experience."* This is the classic playbook of a high-quality compounder whose growth has stalled cyclically: reframe the stall as a deliberate investment phase, give it a memorable four-pillar structure, and put a clock on it ("over the next few years").

**Chapter 3 — "Acceleration is here" (Q4-25, Q1-26).** The forward language turns offensive again. Q1-26: *"Year-over-year contract value growth accelerated in the first quarter"* and the punchline guidance — *"we expect to deliver adjusted EPS on a compound annual basis above 12% over the next 3 years."* The federal headwind is explicitly being lapped (US Fed CV down to ~$114M / ~4% of total). The AI narrative flips from threat-to-defend ("is an LLM replacing Gartner?") to opportunity-to-capture ("highest demand topic," 6,000+ AI documents, AskGartner lifting renewal rates).

**Drivers quietly dropped / added:** the "small tech vendor churn" drag on enterprise count is mentioned every quarter but consistently waved off as low-dollar. The federal business shrank from a renewable base to a ~50%-retention rump and was effectively written down in the narrative. The genuinely *new* driver introduced is the AI-internal-productivity story (content per analyst +31% YoY, publishing time -75%) — a margin and value lever, not yet a revenue one.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Free cash flow well in excess of net income" | every call | TTM FCF $1.26B vs TTM diluted EPS $10.12 (NI ~$0.78B FY25); FCF margin 18.1% | **TRUE & load-bearing.** FCF/NI > 1.6x. This is the real story the EPS line hides. |
| "We repurchased more than $2B of Gartner stock in 2025… reduced share count by 8%" | Q4-25 | Q1-26: *"reduced our share count by about 4%, buying back $535M"* | **TRUE.** ~8% annual share-count reduction at <16x P/E is high-ROI capital return; corroborated quarter-by-quarter. |
| EPS "-39.7% YoY" headline | screener | FY24 EPS $16.0 → FY25 $9.65; but Q1-26 adj EPS $3.32 **+11% YoY**, TTM diluted EPS $10.12 | **OPTICS, not operations.** The FY24→FY25 drop reflects a one-time FY24 benefit (tax/non-operating gain inflating the $16.0 base), NOT a collapse in earning power. Adjusted EPS is *growing double digits*. The peer-comp note's "likely a non-cash charge" instinct is correct in direction. |
| "CV growth accelerated in Q1" / "path back to double-digit" | Q1-26 | Total CV: Q1-25 +7% → Q3-25 +3% → Q4-25 low → **Q1-26 +1% (ex-Fed +3.5%)** | **PARTIALLY TRUE, fragile.** It *did* accelerate sequentially off the trough, but +1% total CV is a long way from the 12–16% target. The acceleration is real but early and small. |
| Gross/contribution margin "stable / strong" | every call | Gross 67.7% stable; Q1-26 total contribution margin **72%**, Insights 77% | **TRUE.** Subscription economics intact; margin guide *down* to 23.5% EBITDA baseline for 2026 is an investment choice, not erosion. |
| ROIC / capital efficiency | Q1-26 | ROIC 21% (screener) / **~27% rolling 4-qtr (mgmt)**; ROE 203.7% | **TRUE & elite.** Asset-light subscription model; ROE is inflated by the buyback-driven thin equity base but ROIC ~21–27% is genuinely high-quality. |

**Where the story is richer than the numbers:** the "clear path back to double-digit growth" has been promised for four straight quarters while CV decelerated from +7% to +1%. The *destination* is credible (the model has done 12–16% historically) but the *timing* has repeatedly slipped right ("more of a '26 and '27 dynamic").

**Where the story is poorer than the numbers:** management *under*-sells the FCF and buyback story. At 9x P/FCF with 8% annual share shrink, the per-share FCF compounding is more powerful than the muted CV-growth narrative conveys. This is the core of the deep-value thesis.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: Declining-then-Stabilizing (a "V" — bottomed Q2/Q3-25, recovering into Q1-26).**

- **Q1-25 (Stable-confident):** *"resilient… ahead of our expectations… eager to repurchase shares aggressively."* Offensive.
- **Q2-25 (Most defensive point):** *"CEO confidence fell to recessionary levels… 78% of CEOs… implementing cost-cutting."* Heavy macro hedging; the segment *rename* (Research→Insights) and the four-dimension framework are the tell of a management reaching for a new narrative because the old growth number stopped working.
- **Q3-25 (Stabilizing):** *"adaptations that are starting to yield results… contract renewal rates improved from the second quarter… repurchased more than $1 billion."* First green shoots language.
- **Q4-25 (Cautiously offensive):** *"well positioned to accelerate CV growth throughout 2026,"* but margin guide steps *down* to 23.5% — they are choosing to invest, signaling confidence in the runway, not the quarter.
- **Q1-26 (Offensive again, with a hedge):** *"contract value growth accelerated… EPS CAGR above 12% over the next 3 years… teams are incredibly optimistic"* — but immediately hedged: *"client decisions slowed somewhat in March"* due to geopolitics, and 2026 selling environment assumed *"no better… than 2025."*

The shift from offensive→defensive→offensive maps cleanly onto the federal/DOGE shock and recovery. Guidance specificity *increased* over time (the explicit 3-year >12% EPS CAGR in Q1-26 is the most concrete forward commitment in the set) — a confidence signal.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: HIGH.** This is the strongest pillar of the dossier.

- The screener records **beat 7/7 quarters (100%)**. Every call in the transcript opens with "ahead of expectations" — Q1-25, Q2-25, Q3-25, Q4-25, Q1-26 all explicitly use that phrase, and Q3-25 / Q1-26 *raised* full-year guidance intra-year.
- The pattern is textbook **sandbagging**: management openly tells you they don't *"build forecasts… based on what we hope is going to happen, but more along what we've seen"* (Q1-25, on federal renewals) — they deliberately exclude upside (e.g. federal clients returning in '26/'27) from guidance. A 7/7 beat rate with explicit conservatism is the signature of a low-ball-and-deliver operator.
- The one credibility watch-item: the *"path back to double-digit CV"* has been a moving target for a year. They have delivered on EPS/FCF/margin guidance every time, but the CV-reacceleration *timing* is the claim most exposed to a future miss. To date, however, sequential CV has in fact inflected up (Q4-25 low → Q1-26 +1% / +3.5% ex-Fed), so even this is tracking.

Net: management says less than it could and delivers more than it says. Score **HIGH**.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"Is an LLM replacing the Gartner subscription?"** — recurring across Q2-25, Q3-25, Q1-26 (Jason Haas, Ashish Sabadra). Management's answer is consistent and, crucially, *data-backed*: they track every cancellation reason and LLM-substitution is *"essentially unmeasurable… a very, very, very small number of transactions."* This is the bear case's load-bearing fear and management meets it with the one thing that rebuts it — loss-reason tracking. Not a deflection.
- **Enterprise client count declining** (Jeff Silber Q2-25; Jasper Bibb Q3-25) — management repeatedly attributes it to small-tech-vendor churn (low-dollar) rather than core-enterprise loss, and the wallet-retention vs client-retention split supports this. A genuine soft spot they keep having to re-explain, but the dollar impact is small.
- **Federal renewal trajectory** (Andrew Nicholas Q1-25) — management modeled ~50% federal retention forward and explicitly excluded any rebound. This is where they were *most* conservative; if federal stabilizes it's pure upside not in numbers.
- **OpEx/margin conservatism** (Q1-25) and **margin step-down to 23.5%** (Q4-25, "is this the new baseline?") — management owns it as a deliberate 2026 investment baseline with margin expansion thereafter. No evasion; they gave the number.

No topic that analysts pushed on later turned into a *negative surprise* in this window — a clean record.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **CV growth decelerated to +1% total (Q1-26)** from +7% (Q1-25) — the franchise *did* stall, and "acceleration" off a +1% base is a low bar; the 12–16% target remains aspirational.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

On one read it's spin; on a more charitable read it widens the addressable framing beyond IT (it now serves "HR, supply chain, finance, marketing, legal"). interrogator_dossier:
# Forensic Dossier — Gartner, Inc. (IT)

*Research & advisory (subscription "Insights" + Consulting). Screener tags it "Industrials" — that is a GICS mislabel; treat it as information services. Transcripts span Q1-2025 → Q1-2026 (six consecutive calls). Signal: deep_value, price ~$142 off a 52-week-low base after a ~52% drawdown from a $337 high.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story degrades in a clean, traceable arc across six quarters — from "resilient, temporary macro" to "structural reset of the growth algorithm."

- **Q1-2025 (May):** Confident, offensive. "Gartner remains resilient in a complex environment." CV +7%. Federal (DOGE) framed as a contained ~4%-of-CV problem; "we expect to reaccelerate CV growth to our target of 12% to 16% when the macroeconomic environment returns to normal." The thesis is *cyclical*: macro is the villain, the algorithm is intact.
- **Q2-2025 (Aug):** First defensive pivot. The CEO opens with **two** takeaways — "AI is an important opportunity" and "we're making *adaptations* that give us a clear path back to double-digit growth." The word "adaptations" enters and never leaves. CEO confidence "fell to recessionary levels… among the fastest drops ever recorded." Research is renamed **"Business and Technology Insights."** Most telling: AskGartner is introduced — an LLM-grounded tool — pre-emptively framing the company on the *right* side of AI. This is the quarter the narrative starts defending the terminal value, not just the cycle.
- **Q3-2025 (Nov):** CV decelerates to **+3%** (+6% ex-Fed). The pitch shifts hard to capital allocation: ">$1 billion of stock" repurchased, share count -6% YoY. AI insight library (6,000+ docs, 1,000+ use cases) is now the lead. The growth story is being *substituted* by a buyback story.
- **Q4-2025 (Feb-2026):** The full "transformation" reveal — four dimensions (impact/volume/timeliness/UX). $2B repurchased in 2025; inaugural IG bond issuance to *lever up for more buyback*; digital markets sale announced. Margin guide steps **down** to a 23.5% "new baseline." Management now concedes "the rate of change and volatility… has increased significantly" and "executives have responded by slowing and deferring everything possible." This is the quarter management stops blaming macro and starts re-architecting the business.
- **Q1-2026 (May):** CV +1% FXN — they spin this as "acceleration from the fourth quarter," but the absolute net new CV went **negative (-$56M)**. The new anchor commitment is financial, not operational: "adjusted EPS on a compound annual basis above 12% over the next 3 years" — an EPS (buyback-driven) promise, conspicuously *not* a CV-growth promise. AskGartner is now free to 100% of users.

**Arc verdict:** A top-line growth story (12–16% CV) has been quietly swapped for a buyback-engineered EPS story, wrapped in an AI-defense narrative. The 12–16% target is still invoked but pushed to "when macro normalizes / medium term / 2027–2028" — the classic receding horizon.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | The financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Clear path back to double-digit growth" | Q2-25→Q1-26 (repeated) | CV growth went **7%→3%→1%**; revenue +3.7% YoY, 3-yr CAGR +5.9% and *decelerating*; net new CV **negative** in Q1-26 | **Diverges hard.** The trend is the opposite of the claim. "Path back" is aspiration, not trajectory. |
| "Free cash flow well in excess of net income" | Every quarter | FCF margin 18.1%, TTM FCF $1.26B vs TTM NI ~$0.73B — **true**, FCF/NI >1.7x | **Corroborated.** This is the genuine quality anchor. |
| "Repurchasing aggressively / $2B in 2025, share count -8%" | Q3-25, Q4-25 | Q1-26 bought $535M, share count -4% YoY; levered up via IG bond to fund it. Net debt **$1.59B** (was net-cash historically) | **Corroborated but financed.** Real buyback, but now **debt-funded** — a quality downgrade in the capital structure. |
| Margins "expand through natural operating leverage" | Q1-25 | EBITDA margin guided **down** to 23.5% "new baseline" (Q4-25); net margin 11.2%; FY25 EPS **-39.7% YoY** (16.0→9.65) | **Diverges.** Operating leverage reversed; FY25 NI fell to $729M from $1.25B. |
| "Beat expectations" (every call) | All | Screener: beat 7/7 qtrs (100%); Q1-26 adj-EPS $3.32 +11%, FCF +29% | **Corroborated — but hollow.** They beat the *lowered* bar while the franchise metric (CV) decayed. Beating EPS via buyback while CV goes negative is the tell. |
| Federal "~4% of CV," contained | Q1-25 | Ex-Fed CV +6–8% vs +1–3% reported — Fed is real, but ex-Fed *also decelerated* | **Partially misleading.** Ex-Fed framing masks that core (commercial) demand also softened by Q1-26 ("client decisions slowed somewhat in March"). |
| AI substitution "essentially unmeasurable," "very, very small" | Q2-25, Q3-25 | Not falsifiable from the P&L; gross margin stable 67.7% so far | **Unverifiable / self-serving.** This is the load-bearing denial. AskGartner being given away free is management *acting* as if the threat is real even as it says the threat is immaterial. |

**Net:** The hard-quality claims (FCF conversion, buyback, gross margin) are TRUE. The growth claims are FALSE-to-date and serially deferred. EPS is being manufactured below the CV line. The narrative is materially **richer** than the trajectory justifies on growth, and the divergence is now the subject of a securities-fraud class action (Feb-4-2025→Feb-2-2026 class period, alleging the CV/Consulting growth claims were misleading).

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING (offensive → defensive), partly re-stabilizing on capital-return confidence.**

- Q1-25 — Offensive: "resilient," "reaccelerate to 12–16%," investing in headcount (+4%).
- Q2-25 — Defensive pivot: "adaptations," "recessionary" CEO confidence, segment rename. Growth language replaced by *transformation* language.
- Q3-25 — Capital-allocation deflection: lead with "$1 billion of stock," not CV.
- Q4-25 — Lowered-bar realism: "slowing and deferring everything," 23.5% "new baseline" margin, levering up. Most candid quarter.
- Q1-26 — Re-confident *on EPS*: "above 12% [EPS] CAGR over the next 3 years" — but note this is a financial-engineering promise, and they admit "client decisions slowed somewhat in March."

Specificity on **CV** guidance fell over time (target became "medium term / when macro normalizes"); specificity on **EPS/FCF/buyback** rose. That mix-shift — vaguer on the operating metric, sharper on the financial metric — is itself a confidence tell.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM.** Two-faced record:
- **Short-cycle guidance: HIGH.** 7/7 EPS beats; they consistently raised EBITDA/EPS/FCF guidance through 2025 and delivered. They under-promise and over-deliver on the *financial* line.
- **Multi-year strategic guidance: LOW.** The "12–16% CV when macro normalizes" framework has been invoked across all six calls without ever being hit; the goalpost moved from "2025 reaccel" → "2026 accel" → "2027–2028 sustained." The securities suit alleges this guidance was not just optimistic but misleading given what they internally saw on consulting/CV. A 100% EPS-beat rate against a CV line going negative is the definition of **sandbagging the bar that's easy to hit while the franchise bar erodes.**

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **LLM substitution** — asked repeatedly (Haas Q2; Sabadra Q2, Q3). Management's answer is consistently "unmeasurable / very, very, very small." This is the topic analysts keep circling and management keeps non-answering with anecdotes ("a client called AskGartner a game changer") rather than data. **The most-deflected question, and the one that matters most for terminal value.**
- **Enterprise/client count declines** (Silber Q2; Bibb Q3) — deflected to "small tech vendor churn." Persistent for years; management never frames it as core-demand weakness.
- **Federal renewal cliff** (Nicholson Q1: ~50% federal dollar retention) — management modeled only what they'd "seen," explicitly not the hoped-for recovery; this later proved to be a genuine drag, validating the analysts' worry.
- **Pricing/discounting** (Silber Q1-26: "offers below the normal $50K ASP?") — flatly denied ("we do not offer discounts"). Worth watching; a denial under direct question is a flag if it later proves wrong.
- **Margin "new baseline"** (George Tong/Sami Q4) — analyst pushed whether 23.5% is structural; CFO conceded "yes… the new baseline." Analysts correctly sniffed a permanent margin reset.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 CV growth collapse 7%→3%→1% FXN with **negative net new CV (-$56M)** in Q1-26 — the franchise growth engine has stalled, not paused.
🔴 **Securities-fraud class action** (lead-plaintiff deadline May-18-2026, now passed) alleging the CV/Consulting growth claims were misleading; stock fell from $337 to ~$143 on two corrective disclosures (-27.5% Aug-5-25; -20.9% Feb-3-26).
🔴 EPS growth now **buyback-manufactured** and **debt-financed** (IG bond issued specifically to lever up for repurchase; net debt $1.59B where the franchise historically ran net-cash-like).
🔴 Margin reset to a **23.5% "new baseline"** — management's own words; operating leverage running in reverse.
🔴 **AskGartner given away free to 100% of users** — the clearest tell that management privately treats LLM substitution as a real ARPU/pricing-power threat, even while publicly calling it "unmeasurable."
🔴 Segment rename (Research → "Insights") mid-deceleration — cosmetic re-labeling during bad news.
🟢 Genuine FCF quality: TTM FCF $1.26B, FCF/NI >1.7x, FCF +29% YoY in Q1-26.
🟢 Stable 67.7% gross margin and ~27% rolling ROIC — no margin erosion *yet*; the moat is intact on the cost side.
🟢 Subscription model real: multiyear contracts "with no out clauses," 80%+ recurring, client retention +~100bps YoY.
🟢 Disciplined, price-sensitive buyback at a 52-week-low valuation — accretive if the franchise holds.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Metric substitution:** the headline commitment migrated from a *CV* target (operating health) to an *EPS CAGR* target (financial engineering). When a subscription business stops guiding to contract value and starts guiding to per-share earnings, the recurring engine is the problem.
- **"Adaptations / transformation"** became the dominant noun — language that signals the old playbook stopped working.
- **Free AskGartner** = revealed preference. You don't give away the AI layer for free if you're confident clients will pay more for it; you do it to *defend retention* against substitution. Management's actions contradict its "immaterial" words.
- **Levering a historically un-levered balance sheet to buy back stock** at the lows is a high-conviction bet by management that the franchise is mispriced — but it also removes the net-cash cushion if the bear case (terminal AI erosion) is right.
- The digital-markets sale ($105M) is a rounding error dressed as a "shareholder-value action" — narrative padding.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (toward financial engineering) over a DETERIORATING operating base.** Gartner is pivoting from organic-growth-led value creation to buyback-led EPS creation, financed with new debt, while the core CV engine has stalled to ~zero net growth.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** *Is the AskGartner/LLM substitution risk a manageable workflow-defense (in which case ~8.7x P/FCF on a 67% gross-margin subscription compounder is a generational fear-print) or the leading edge of structural ARPU/retention erosion (in which case a low multiple on a slowly-shrinking, debt-levered base is a value trap)?* The stable gross margin says "not yet eroding"; the free-AskGartner action and negative net new CV say "the market is right to worry." The buyback is only accretive if it's the former.

**Moat assessment.** Moat type = **intangible-brand + switching-cost** (proprietary data flywheel: 500k+ annual client conversations, multiyear no-out-clause contracts, embedded workflow in CIO/CxO decision-making). Width by the *evidence*: returns remain elevated (ROIC ~21–27%, gross margin 67.7% *stable*), which is **WIDE** on levels — but the **trend is ERODING**: CV growth 7%→1%, net new CV negative, margin reset to a lower baseline, and the company defensively giving away its AI layer. A wide-but-narrowing moat. **Secular threat = MATERIAL (not yet terminal):** generative-AI displacement of syndicated research/advisory is the live structural force — LLMs collapse the marginal cost of "good-enough" research and cap pricing power for incumbents whose moat is *workflow habit* more than hard lock-in. Gartner's hard-lock elements (proprietary primary data, executive peer network, multiyear contracts) are real and blunt the threat to "material," not "terminal" — but they do not eliminate it, and the gross margin is the tripwire to watch. The cheapness is **partly a fear-print and partly a melting-base discount**; you cannot credit it as pure mispricing.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING | MOAT: WIDE | MOAT_TREND: ERODING | SECULAR_THREAT: material

bull_thesis: Gartner is a ~78%-recurring subscription research franchise (Insights segment) trading at P/FCF 7.8x and ~12-13x trailing/forward EPS after a ~77% drawdown from the Nov-2024 ATH of $552 to ~$127 — a fear-print on a 21%-ROIC, 67.7%-gross-margin business that generates FCF *above* net income (TTM FCF $1.26B vs NI ~$0.8B). The bear case is AI/LLM substitution, but the live evidence cuts the other way: enterprise client retention rose ~100bps YoY through the worst of the macro/DOGE-federal hit (Q4'25), management tracks the reason for every cancellation and states LLM-citing churn is 'essentially unmeasurable / a very, very, very small number of transactions' (Q2-Q3'25), and AskGartner — rolled to 100% of licensed users — drove 'substantially higher renewal rates' for users vs non-users (Q4'25), i.e. AI is so far a retention TAILWIND, not a leak. Capital allocation is aggressively pro-shareholder: $2B repurchased in FY25 (−6% share count YoY), an IG bond issued to fund MORE buyback into the trough, and the non-core Digital Markets unit divested. Conditions that must hold: (1) retention stays flat-to-up (not the first leg of structural leakage); (2) CV reaccelerates off the federal lap in 2026; (3) the 23.5% margin baseline holds. If so, fair value is well north of $180 on a normalized 15-18x EPS / 12x FCF, with a re-rate as growth visibly inflects.
bear_thesis: This is a structurally-impaired franchise whose moat is workflow habit and brand-as-reference — exactly the layer generative AI collapses — and the chart already knows it: −77% from the ATH is not a macro air-pocket, it is the early innings of a terminal re-rating of syndicated research/advisory toward a permanently lower multiple. The tells are in management's own mouth: CV growth has *decelerated* 7% (Q1'25) → 3% (Q3'25) → +1% FX-neutral (Q1'26 actual) despite a year of 'adaptations yielding results' — the inflection keeps not arriving; the margin baseline was *cut* to 23.5% and Safian explicitly called it the 'new baseline' (Q4'25), conceding the cost of competing (more analysts, more content, AI tooling) has structurally risen; AskGartner is *bundled at no incremental charge* — an AI defense that is pure cost with zero monetization, capping ARPU just as 3-4% price increases are already the ceiling. The 'LLM churn is unmeasurable' answer is a non-falsifiable deflection of the precise risk the whole thesis hinges on — when management won't quantify it, assume it is being managed, not measured. FY25 EPS fell −39.7% YoY and the 7/7 beat streak is a beat of a *lowered* bar (they beat a base that halved). The enterprise client count is declining (GTS then GBS), waved off as 'small tech vendor churn' that conveniently insulates the core. Most dangerous: they are *levering up* (IG bond) to buy back stock into a falling knife — if the base is melting, this is financial engineering into a value trap that removes the balance-sheet cushion (net debt now $1.59B). What confirms the bear: any quarter where retention rolls over, CV stays sub-3%, or ARPU/pricing concedes — at which point a 'cheap' 7.8x FCF re-rates to 5-6x on a shrinking base and the buyback compounds the loss per share.
consensus_delta: For a deep_value name the question is whether the street is pricing terminal decline into a franchise with stable/growing earnings power. The street IS looking the wrong way in BOTH directions, partially offsetting. The sell-side target ($176, +38%) and consensus fwd EPS growth +12.2% still model a clean cyclical reacceleration — the legacy 'Gartner always compounds back to 12-16% CV' anchor — which the live data (CV +1% FX-neutral, Q1'26; analyst-target velocity −24%; margin baseline cut to 23.5%) does NOT yet support; that consensus is too optimistic on the *path*. Simultaneously, the *price* (7.8x FCF, ~12x EPS, −77% from ATH) is pricing structural AI-displacement terminal decline that the evidence — retention +100bps, FCF>NI, AskGartner *raising* renewal rates, LLM churn 'unmeasurable' — does not corroborate; that is too pessimistic on the *terminal*. The expectations-arbitrage edge is real but two-sided and self-cancelling: you are long a mispriced terminal multiple but short a too-rosy near-term growth path. interrogator_dossier:
# Forensic Dossier — Gartner, Inc. (IT)

*Signal type: deep_value · Sector (screener): Industrials [misclassified — IT is a subscription research/advisory franchise, NOT industrials] · Transcripts: Q1'25 → Q4'25 (FY25 cycle)*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Across the four FY2025 calls (May, Aug, Nov 2025, Feb 2026) Gartner's story underwent a clear and consistent **arc from "resilient compounder pausing" to "structurally transforming under siege."**

- **Q1'25 (May):** The frame was *resilience + temporary macro.* Contract value (CV) grew 7%; management leaned on a litany of past shocks survived ("COVID, tech bubble, 40-yr inflation, ground war in Europe") and positioned the U.S. federal/DOGE hit as a contained ~4%-of-CV problem. The promise: "reaccelerate CV growth to our target of 12% to 16% when the macroeconomic environment returns to normal." The thesis was *wait for normalization.*
- **Q2'25 (Aug):** The pivot to AI as the dominant lens begins. Hall opens with "two things": (1) "AI is an important opportunity for Gartner" and (2) "we're making adaptations that give us a clear path back to double-digit growth." This is the first call where management feels compelled to **pre-empt the AI-substitution bear case** — rolling out AskGartner, citing CEO-confidence "at recessionary levels," and recharacterizing the *Research* segment as *Business and Technology Insights ("Insights").* A name change to a flagship segment mid-crisis is a tell.
- **Q3'25 (Nov):** CV growth decelerates to **3% (6% ex-Fed)**. The narrative is now "operational adaptations are starting to yield results" — engagement up, retention up ~100bps, $1B+ buyback. The AI defense hardens: "6,000 AI documents," "1,000 use cases," AskGartner rolled to 100% of users. Notably the *offensive* "12–16%" target is now hedged to "double-digit growth over the medium term."
- **Q4'25 (Feb'26):** Full transformation framing — "the world is changing more than ever; this is a huge opportunity for us." Margin guidance steps DOWN to a new **23.5% baseline** (from ~25%), $2B FY25 buyback, an investment-grade bond issued to **lever up for more buyback**, and the **Digital Markets divestiture** announced. The story has quietly migrated from *growth franchise* to *cash-return / financial-engineering franchise buying time for an operational turnaround.*

**Dropped quietly:** the crisp "12–16% CV when macro normalizes." **Introduced:** AskGartner, the "Insights" rebrand, the 4-dimension ("impact/volume/timeliness/user-experience") transformation, and leverage-funded buyback.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter | The hard numbers | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Clear path back to double-digit growth" | Q2–Q4'25 | CV growth 7% → 3% (Q3) → +1% FX-neutral total CV (Q1'26 actual, $5.3B). Revenue +3.7% YoY, decelerating | **Poorer than narrated.** CV is *decelerating,* not inflecting. Double-digit is a hope, not a trend. |
| "Resilient... results ahead of expectations" / beat every quarter | All | EPS beat 7/7 qtrs (100%); but FY25 EPS **9.65, −39.7% YoY**; NI 1.25B (FY24) → 729M (FY25) | **Mixed.** Beats are real but against a *collapsing* base. The −40% EPS drop swamps the beats — they beat a lowered bar. (Note: FY24 EPS 16.0 / NI 1.25B was inflated by one-offs; normalized TTM EPS is ~10.1.) |
| "We generate FCF well in excess of net income" | All | TTM FCF 1.26B vs TTM NI ~0.8B; FCF margin 18.1%; P/FCF 7.8x | **TRUE and the load-bearing fact.** FCF conversion >NI is the genuine quality marker. This is the real value anchor, not GAAP EPS. |
| "Repurchased $2B of stock in 2025... eager to repurchase aggressively, price-sensitive" | Q3–Q4'25 | $1B+ (Q3) and $1.1B (Q3 alone), $2B FY; share count −6% YoY; **issued IG debt to fund it** | **TRUE but financed.** Net debt 1.59B (recomputed). They are levering to shrink the share count into a falling stock — accretive IF earnings power is stable, value-destructive if it is structurally eroding. |
| Margins "expand through natural operating leverage" | Q1'25 | Margin guide *stepped DOWN* to 23.5% "new baseline" by Q4'25 | **CONTRADICTED within the year.** The Q1 operating-leverage promise was reversed to a lower baseline by Q4. |
| ROE 203.7% / ROIC 21.0% | financials | ROE distorted by buyback-shrunk equity (near-zero/negative book); ROIC 21% is real | ROE is a **financial-engineering artifact** (treasury stock), ignore it. ROIC 21% is the honest, still-premium return — but it is *falling* from prior-year levels as EPS halved. |

**Net:** The numbers support a *high-FCF, high-ROIC subscription franchise mid-air-pocket,* not a broken business — but they do NOT support the "clear path back to double-digit growth" claim, which remains unproven and is decelerating in the live data.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY — **DECLINING (offensive → defensive)**

- **Q1'25 (confident/offensive):** "We'll continue to be agile... invest to support long-term sustained double-digit growth." Past-shocks-survived bravado.
- **Q2'25 (defensive pivot):** "Measures of CEO confidence fell to recessionary levels, among the fastest drops ever recorded"; "78% of CEOs implementing cost-cutting." Tone shifts to explaining headwinds and **pre-bunking AI substitution.**
- **Q3'25 (cautiously reassuring):** "adaptations are starting to yield results" — but the proof points are *leading indicators* (engagement, pipeline "up double digits"), not booked CV, which fell to 3%.
- **Q4'25 (resigned-but-reframing):** "Executives have responded by slowing and deferring everything possible. This makes for a much tougher selling environment." "Some say the rate of change will never be this slow again." Margin baseline *lowered.* The language is now about *surviving a structurally harder sell* and returning cash — a defensive posture dressed as opportunity.

Guidance specificity *decreased* on growth (the 12–16% target was de-anchored to "medium term") while *increasing* on capital return (precise buyback dollars). That asymmetry — vague on the top line, precise on financial engineering — is the classic late-cycle defensive tell.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY — **MEDIUM**

- **Beat rate 7/7 (100%)** on EPS is genuine and supports near-term *sandbagging discipline* (Safian repeatedly frames OpEx guides as "our normal prudent approach," and they consistently came in "ahead of expectations"). On *near-term* numbers, credibility is HIGH.
- **BUT** the *strategic* guidance has a credibility gap: the Q1'25 "operating leverage → margin expansion" was reversed to a *lower 23.5% baseline* by Q4'25, and the "12–16% CV" target has been pushed out every call ("2027, 2028 and beyond"). Forward-looking strategic promises have NOT been delivered on the timeline implied.
- Net reliability: **MEDIUM** — trust them on the quarter, discount them on the multi-year reacceleration.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

The single recurring pressure point, asked four different ways across the year, is **LLM/AI substitution risk** — and management's answers are the most evasive part of the transcripts:

- Q2'25 (Haas): "what percentage of folks are citing usage of a publicly available LLM... not consuming the Gartner subscription?" → Hall: *"it's not material. It's essentially unmeasurable."*
- Q3'25 (Sabadra): "how often does LLM come up as an alternative to Gartner?" → Hall: *"extremely small... a very, very, very small number of transactions."*
- Q3'25 (Bibb): pressing whether AskGartner is a separate charge / engagement → confirmed bundled into base package (i.e. it generates **no incremental revenue** — a defensive retention tool, a cost, not a growth lever).

The "essentially unmeasurable" / "very very very small" answers are **non-falsifiable deflections.** When management can't or won't quantify the precise risk the entire bear thesis hinges on, that is itself a yellow flag. The *second* pressure point — declining enterprise client count (GTS then GBS) — management attributes entirely to "small tech vendor churn," which is plausible but conveniently insulates the core franchise from blame.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 CV growth decelerated 7%→3%→+1% FX-neutral despite "adaptations yielding results."
🔴 Margin baseline *cut* from ~25% to 23.5% — admitted structural, not cyclical, by Safian Q4'25.
🔴 Segment **rebranded mid-crisis** (Research → "Insights") — cosmetic moves under pressure.
🔴 **Levering up (IG bond) to buy back stock into a −77% drawdown** — high-conviction capital return, but it removes the balance-sheet cushion if the air-pocket is structural.
🔴 The AI-substitution question is met with "unmeasurable / very very very small" — unquantified deflection of the core risk.
🔴 AskGartner is **bundled (no incremental revenue)** — an AI defense that is pure cost.

🟢 FCF conversion **>net income** consistently; TTM FCF 1.26B at P/FCF 7.8x is genuinely cheap.
🟢 ROIC 21%, gross margin 67.7% (stable), Insights ~78% recurring subscription with multiyear, no-out-clause contracts (Safian Q1'25: "true multiyear contracts... no out clauses").
🟢 Retention *up ~100bps YoY* (Q4'25) even through the worst of the macro/Fed hit.
🟢 Beat 7/7 quarters; latest-Q EPS **+17% YoY** (TTM diluted EPS 10.12) — the trough may be in.
🟢 6% YoY share-count reduction — if earnings power holds, the per-share math is powerful.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The "Insights" rebrand** is not cosmetic-only: by Q3'25 they also moved "most Insights non-subscription revenue to *other* revenue," *purifying* the headline Insights segment to "almost 100% recurring." This is management curating the metric investors watch to emphasize recurring quality precisely as growth stalls — defensible, but a signaling choice.
- **Capital-allocation tone shift:** from "price-sensitive, opportunistic, disciplined" buyback (Q1) to *issuing debt to fund $2B* (Q4). They are telling you they think the stock is cheap with conviction — but also that organic growth isn't absorbing the cash.
- **"The new baseline is 23.5%"** (Safian, Q4'25) — management itself reclassifying the margin step-down as *permanent,* not a dip to be recovered. A subtle but important admission that the cost of competing (more analysts, more content, AI tooling) has structurally risen.
- **AI framed offensively, deployed defensively:** every AI mention is "opportunity," but every concrete AI product (AskGartner) is bundled and aimed at *retention,* not new pricing/ARPU. The gap between the offensive language and the defensive deployment is the tell.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT — **PIVOTING (compounder → cash-return / defend-the-base)**

The trajectory is PIVOTING: a former double-digit growth compounder is mid-transition to a *defend-the-recurring-base, return-all-cash* franchise, funding aggressive buyback with new leverage while it waits for an operational turnaround that has so far only shown up in leading indicators, not booked CV.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *Is the ~77% drawdown a fear-print on a temporarily air-pocketed but durable subscription franchise (P/FCF 7.8x of a 21%-ROIC, 78%-recurring compounder), or is it the early innings of an AI-displacement re-rating of syndicated research toward a structurally lower terminal multiple?* The buyback-funded leverage means the downside is NOT symmetric — if the base is melting, they are levering into a value trap.

**ECONOMIC MOAT:** Type = **intangible-brand + switching-cost** (the Gartner brand as the CYA standard-of-reference in enterprise IT procurement — "Magic Quadrant" is the buying-committee default — plus multiyear no-out-clause subscriptions and embedded peer-network/analyst-access habit). WIDTH: the *level* is wide (67.7% gross margin, 21% ROIC, retention +100bps), but the EVIDENCE shows it is **ERODING at the margin** — CV growth collapsing to ~1%, margin baseline cut to 23.5%, ARPU/pricing power capped (3–4% price increases are the ceiling; AskGartner bundled with no incremental monetization). A high-but-no-longer-rising return profile with a *lowered* margin baseline is an ERODING moat, not a widening one.

**SECULAR THREAT (terminal-value test):** The structural force is **AI-displacement of syndicated research/advisory** — LLMs collapse the marginal cost of "good-enough" research output, capping the pricing power of a franchise whose moat is *workflow habit and brand-as-reference* rather than hard data lock-in. This is the textbook ai-displacement victim layer ("syndicated research & advisory"). The threat is real and **material** — but NOT yet demonstrably *terminal:* the moat is brand/trust/independence (the Magic Quadrant as a procurement-defensibility artifact, proprietary 500k+ annual client conversations, conference network) which LLMs trained on public data cannot trivially replicate, and retention is *rising,* not falling. The cheapness is therefore a **partial fear-print over a genuinely threatened base** — neither a clean mispricing nor a confirmed value trap. The evidence (rising retention, FCF>NI, no measurable LLM-driven churn per management) tilts it toward *material-not-terminal,* but the eroding moat-trend and lowered margin baseline mean it cannot be underwritten as WIDE.
